title: Entryway Cabinet with Hooks
summary: A tall plywood upper cabinet with three inset doors and a hook rail — designed to mount above a drawer bench, but a complete standalone build on its own.
description:
Inset doors are the one real step up in difficulty from a typical overlay-door cabinet: instead of a door that simply covers the opening from the front, an inset door has to fit *inside* the opening with an even gap all around, which means the case has to be square and the doors have to be sized precisely — there's no overlay lip to hide a sloppy fit. Build the face frame as a separate flat assembly first, exactly like a simpler cabinet, but expect to spend real time fine-tuning each door before you commit to final hinge mortises.

The hook rail is a structural detail worth taking seriously, not just an accessory: it's a horizontal board mounted at the back of one shelf bay specifically to carry hook hardware, and it's called out for extra backing support because coats and bags loaded onto hooks put real leverage on that one board in a way that a shelf carrying its own weight doesn't experience. If you're mounting this over a bench, that back support also matters for how the whole assembly transfers weight down to the bench top.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into 15-3/4" wide, 8 ft strips. 1x12 boards work too, at a shallower depth.

cutList.note: Extra support behind any shelf that will carry hook loads.
Build each door to fit its actual opening.

steps.title: Attach the shelves
steps.body: Drill three 3/4" pocket holes on each end of every shelf board and attach to the sides with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. If this cabinet will sit on top of a bench, also drill pocket holes on the bottom of the sides for that connection.

steps.title: Add the hook rail
steps.body: Drill two pocket holes on each end of the 1x6 and 1x4 boards and attach to the sides. Nail the bottom shelf to the top edge of the middle board where the hooks will go, using 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. If the top two shelves will carry heavy loads, add a 1x3 backing strip under their back edges too — the same reinforcement the hook rail gets.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame as its own flat assembly with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, and glue — easier to keep square on the bench than in place on the cabinet. Attach it to the front of the cabinet with brad nails and glue, nailing through into the shelves for a solid connection.

steps.title: Build and fit the three inset doors
steps.body: Build each door frame to fit its actual opening, not the plan's numbers verbatim — an inset door needs a consistent, even gap all the way around, and that only happens if you fit each door to what you actually built rather than what the plan says you should have built. Mortise the inset hinges once you're happy with the fit, and add a magnetic catch to each door.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and add hardware
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, finish, then add knobs and the wall hooks last.

images.alt: Entryway upper cabinet with inset doors and a hook rail
